perturbation.original_text: 10.2 No Joint Venture. The Parties agree that and acknowledge that the relationship of the Parties is in the nature of an independent contractor. This Agreement shall not be deemed to create a partnership or joint venture and neither Party is the other's agent, partner, employee or representative. Neither Party shall have any right, power or authority to enter into any agreement for or on behalf of, or to assume or create any obligation, liability, or responsibility on behalf of the other.
perturbation.changed_text: 10.2 No Joint Venture. The Parties agree that and acknowledge that the relationship of the Parties is in the nature of an independent contractor. This Agreement shall be deemed to create a partnership and joint venture, and each Party is considered the other's agent, partner, employee or representative. Each Party shall have full right, power and authority to enter into any agreement for or on behalf of, or to assume or create any obligation, liability, or responsibility on behalf of the other.
perturbation.explanation: The original text explicitly denies the creation of a partnership or joint venture, typical for independent contractor agreements. The modified text contradicts this by stating that the agreement *does* create such a relationship and that each party is the other's agent, partner, employee or representative, which creates a contradictory legal standing.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text asserts that the agreement creates a partnership, directly contradicting the definition of a partnership under federal law. Specifically, 26 U.S.C. § 7701(a)(2) defines partnership to include various collaborative business relationships. The modification forces the independent contractor agreement to have components of partnership despite initial agreement.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 26 U.S. Code § 7701 - Definitions | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 26 U.S. Code § 7701 - Definitions U.S. Code Notes Authorities (CFR) prev | next (a) When used in this title, where not otherwise distinctly expressed or manifestly incompatible with the intent thereof— (1) Person The term “ person ” shall be construed to mean and include an individual, a trust, estate, partnership , association, company or corporation. (2) Partnership and partner The term “ partnership ” includes a syndicate, group, pool, joint venture, or other unincorporated organization, through or by means of which any business, financial operation, or venture is carried on, and which is not, within the meaning of this title, a trust or estate or a corporation; and the term “partner” includes a member in such a syndicate, group, pool, joint venture, or organization. (3) Corporation The term “ corporation ” includes associations, joint -stock companies, and insurance companies. (4) Domestic The term “ domestic ” when applied to a corporation or partnership means created or organized in the United States or under the law of the United States or of any State unless, in the case of a partnership, the Secretary provides otherwise by regulations. (5) Foreign The term “ foreign ” when applied to a corporation or partnership means a corporation or partnership which is not domestic. (6) Fiduciary The term “ fiduciary ” means a guardian, trustee, executor, administrator, receiver, conservator, or any person acting in any fiduciary capacity for any person. (7) Stock The term “ stock ” includes shares in an association, joint- stock company, or insurance company. (8) Shareholder The term “ shareholder ” includes a member in an association, joint -stock company, or insurance company. (9) United States The term “ United States ” when used in a geographical sense includes only the States and the District of Columbia
perturbation.justification: The changed text explicitly states that the agreement creates a partnership and joint venture, directly contradicting the original text, which states the opposite. This creates a legal contradiction because it redefines the relationship between the parties, potentially imposing partnership liabilities and responsibilities where none were intended. According to 26 U.S.C. § 7701 (a)(2), the term “partnership” includes a syndicate, group, pool, joint venture, or other unincorporated organization, through or by means of which any business, financial operation, or venture is carried on. By stating the agreement creates a partnership, the changed text brings the agreement under this definition despite the original intention of an independent contractor relationship, creating a misaligned terminology legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 3.1 The Company shall pay HDI thirty percent (3016) of the Net Sales in excess of Eleven Thousand Dollars ($11,000) per calendar month. "Net Sales" shall mean all revenues from the sale of Deerskin Products on the Site including shipping and handling charges, minus refunds and exchanges.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.1 The Company shall pay HDI thirty percent (3016) of the Net Sales in excess of Eleven Thousand Dollars ($11,000) per calendar month. "Net Sales" shall mean all revenues from the sale of Deerskin Products on the Site, excluding shipping and handling charges, including refunds and exchanges.
perturbation.explanation: The original text defines Net Sales as revenues including shipping and handling and minus refunds/exchanges. The modified text inverts this by excluding shipping and handling charges but including refunds and exchanges. This changes the basis of payment, creating an ambiguity in how revenue is calculated, and therefore creates contradiction in payment terms.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification alters the 'Net Sales' definition, which has implications for determining revenue from sales, particularly in relation to shipping terms. UCC 2-319 defines the meaning of shipping terms, especially who bears responsibility for shipping costs. The amended language conflicts with standard interpretations of sales contracts that include shipping costs in total revenue, potentially shifting liability without clear agreement.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-319. F.O.B. and F.A.S. Terms. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-319. F.O.B. and F.A.S. Terms. (1)Unless otherwise agreed the term F.O.B. (which means "free on board") at a named place, even though used only in connection with the stated price, is a delivery term under which (a) when the term is F.O.B. the place of shipment, the seller must at that place ship the goods in the manner provided in this Article (Section 2-504 ) and bear the expense and risk of putting them into the possession of the carrier; or (b) when the term is F.O.B. the place of destination, the seller must at his own expense and risk transport the goods to that place and there tender delivery of them in the manner provided in this Article (Section 2-503 ); (c) when under either (a) or (b) the term is also F.O.B. vessel, car or other vehicle, the seller must in addition at his own expense and risk load the goods on board. If the term is F.O.B. vessel the buyer must name the vessel and in an appropriate case the seller must comply with the provisions of this Article on the form of bill of lading (Section 2-323 ). (2) Unless otherwise agreed the term F.A.S. vessel (which means "free alongside") at a named port, even though used only in connection with the stated price, is a delivery term under which the seller must (a) at his own expense and risk deliver the goods alongside the vessel in the manner usual in that port or on a dock designated and provided by the buyer ; and (b) obtain and tender a receipt for the goods in exchange for which the carrier is under a duty to issue a bill of lading. (3) Unless otherwise agreed in any case falling within subsection (1)(a) or (c) or subsection (2) the buyer must seasonably give any needed instructions for making delivery, including when the term is F.A.S. or F.O.B. the loading berth of the vessel and in an appropriate case its name and sailing date. The seller may treat the
perturbation.justification: The contract modification alters the definition of 'Net Sales' by excluding shipping and handling charges while including refunds and exchanges, directly contradicting the original definition. The LLM explanation points out that this change has implications for determining revenue from sales, particularly in relation to shipping terms. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 regarding UCC 2-319 primarily discusses F.O.B. and F.A.S. terms, it highlights the importance of clearly defined shipping terms in sales contracts. The modification introduces an ambiguity in how revenue is calculated, leading to a contradiction in payment terms, which falls under the Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction definition, as the contractual language deviates from legally recognized definitions (or at least, deviates from the originally agreed upon definition). Therefore, the modification weakens the original contractual obligation.

perturbation.original_text: 5.3 The Company shall be deemed the "merchant of record" for all commercial transactions on the Site related to Deerskin Products. Until the sale of the Deerskin Products to the consumer from the Site all title to the Deerskin Products shall remain with the Company.
perturbation.changed_text: 5.3 The Company shall not be deemed the "merchant of record" for all commercial transactions on the Site related to Deerskin Products. Until the sale of the Deerskin Products to the consumer from the Site all title to the Deerskin Products shall transfer to HDI.
perturbation.explanation: By negating that the Company is the "merchant of record" and changing ownership to HDI prior to sale, the modification causes confusion about who is responsible for sales tax collection, compliance with consumer protection laws, and product liability. It inverts the standard practice where the product seller retains title until purchase.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification affects product liability under the Magnuson-Moss Warranty Act (15 U.S.C. § 2304). By transferring title to HDI before sale, it creates an ambiguity as to who is responsible for implied warranties. Typically, the merchant of record would bear this responsibility. This contradiction could render warranty obligations uncertain.

Federal minimum standards for warranties (a) Remedies under written warranty; duration of implied warranty; exclusion or limitation on consequential damages for breach of written or implied warranty; election of refund or replacement In order for a warrantor warranting a consumer product by means of a written warranty to meet the Federal minimum standards for warranty- (1) such warrantor must as a minimum remedy such consumer product within a reasonable time and without char
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 15 U.S. Code § 2304 - Federal minimum standards for warranties | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 15 U.S. Code § 2304 - Federal minimum standards for warranties U.S. Code prev | next (a) Remedies under written warranty; duration of implied warranty; exclusion or limitation on consequential damages for breach of written or implied warranty; election of refund or replacement In order for a warrantor warranting a consumer product by means of a written warranty to meet the Federal minimum standards for warranty— (1) such warrantor must as a minimum remedy such consumer product within a reasonable time and without charge , in the case of a defect, malfunction, or failure to conform with such written warranty; (2) notwithstanding section 2308(b) of this title , such warrantor may not impose any limitation on the duration of any implied warranty on the product; (3) such warrantor may not exclude or limit consequential damages for breach of any written or implied warranty on such product, unless such exclusion or limitation conspicuously appears on the face of the warranty; and (4) if the product (or a component part thereof) contains a defect or malfunction after a reasonable number of attempts by the warrantor to remedy defects or malfunctions in such product, such warrantor must permit the consumer to elect either a refund for, or replacement without charge of, such product or part (as the case may be). The Commission may by rule specify for purposes of this paragraph, what constitutes a reasonable number of attempts to remedy particular kinds of defects or malfunctions under different circumstances. If the warrantor replaces a component part of a consumer product, such replacement shall include installing the part in the product without charge . (b) Duties and conditions imposed on consumer by warrantor (1) In fulfilling the duties under subsection (a) respecting a written war
perturbation.justification: The modification creates a contradiction by shifting the responsibility related to the product warranty away from the company. The original text implies the Company is the warrantor, but the changed text, by designating HDI as the title holder prior to sale and denying the Company is the 'merchant of record,' creates ambiguity about who the warrantor is. This directly undermines the requirements of 15 U.S.C. § 2304, which outlines the 'Federal minimum standards for warranties.' Specifically, §2304(a) states that 'In order for a warrantor warranting a consumer product by means of a written warranty to meet the Federal minimum standards for warranty,' the warrantor must fulfill certain obligations. By obscuring who the warrantor is, the contract conflicts with this statute.
